name: PulsePilot
tagline: Turn symptoms into smart next steps.
description: PulsePilot is a symptom-navigation app that helps people decide what to do when they feel unwell, without guessing, panic-searching, or overusing urgent care. Instead of offering generic symptom lists, it asks a short adaptive series of questions, factors in medical history, medications, and recent activity, and then translates the result into a clear action plan like self-care, same-day clinic visit, telehealth, or emergency care. It solves the common problem of not knowing whether a symptom is minor, urgent, or just needs monitoring, which can lead to delayed treatment, unnecessary ER trips, or anxiety spirals. In daily life, it acts like a calm triage companion: a parent can check a child’s fever after school, an older adult can assess dizziness after a new prescription, or a person with asthma can judge whether wheezing is becoming dangerous. Its unique twist is a "what changed?" timeline that compares the current issue to prior episodes and shows likely triggers, medication interactions, and red-flag combinations in plain language. The app also generates a concise, shareable summary for doctors so users arrive prepared instead of trying to remember details while stressed. Unlike standard symptom checkers, PulsePilot is built around decision support and context, not diagnosis theater.
features: An adaptive triage engine asks only the most relevant questions and converts the answers into clear next-step guidance, which reduces confusion and helps users act appropriately faster.

A personal medical context layer stores medications, chronic conditions, allergies, and recent changes so the app can flag interactions or higher-risk situations that generic symptom checkers often miss.

A symptom timeline lets users compare the current episode with past ones, making it easier to notice patterns such as recurring migraine triggers, post-workout chest tightness, or side effects after starting a new drug.
A plain-language red-flag detector highlights when symptoms require urgent care and explains why, which helps users understand the severity instead of relying on vague alarms.
A visit-prep summary creates a doctor-ready snapshot of symptoms, onset, duration, severity, and possible triggers so users can communicate efficiently during telehealth or in-person appointments.
audience: Adults 18+, parents, caregivers, and people managing chronic conditions who need fast, trustworthy symptom guidance at home or on the go. It is especially useful for users in busy, high-stress situations who want a safer alternative to internet searching or unclear self-triage.
useCases: Sarah just finished a stressful meeting and notices her heart is racing and she feels lightheaded. She opens PulsePilot to check whether it looks like stress, dehydration, or something that needs urgent attention.

Marcus started a new antibiotic two days ago and now has a strange rash on his arms. He uses the app to compare the timing, medication list, and symptoms so he knows whether to call his doctor immediately.

Leila’s child develops a fever late at night, and she cannot tell if it is safe to wait until morning. She enters the temperature, recent symptoms, and the child’s age to get a clear recommendation and a shareable note for the pediatrician.
